coredata.dc:title: Bacterial detection based on Förster resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wanqing
Li, Weiqiang
Song, Yang
Xu, Qian
Xu, Hengyi
coredata.dc:description: The huge economic loss and threat to human health caused by bacterial infection have attracted the public's concern, and there is an urgent need to relieve and improve the tough problem. Therefore, it is significant to establish a facile, rapid, and sensitive method for bacterial detection considering the shortcomings of existing methods. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensors have exhibited immense potential and applicability for bacterial detection given their high signal-to-noise ratio and high sensitivity. This review focuses on the development of FRET-based fluorescence assays for bacterial detection. We summarize the principle of FRET-based assays, discuss the commonly used recognition molecules and further introduce three frequent construction strategies. Based on the strategies and materials, relevant applications are presented. Moreover, some restrictions of FRET fluorescence sensors and development prospects are discussed. Suitable donor-acceptor pairs and stable recognition molecules are the essential conditions for sensors to play their roles, and there is still some room for development. Besides, applying FRET fluorescence sensors to point-of-care detection is still difficult. Future developments could focus on near-infrared fluorescent dyes and simultaneous detection of multiple analytes.

head.ce:title: Bacterial detection based on Förster resonance energy transfer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The huge economic loss and threat to human health caused by bacterial infection have attracted the public's concern, and there is an urgent need to relieve and improve the tough problem. Therefore, it is significant to establish a facile, rapid, and sensitive method for bacterial detection considering the shortcomings of existing methods. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensors have exhibited immense potential and applicability for bacterial detection given their high signal-to-noise ratio and high sensitivity. This review focuses on the development of FRET-based fluorescence assays for bacterial detection. We summarize the principle of FRET-based assays, discuss the commonly used recognition molecules and further introduce three frequent construction strategies. Based on the strategies and materials, relevant applications are presented. Moreover, some restrictions of FRET fluorescence sensors and development prospects are discussed. Suitable donor-acceptor pairs and stable recognition molecules are the essential conditions for sensors to play their roles, and there is still some room for development. Besides, applying FRET fluorescence sensors to point-of-care detection is still difficult. Future developments could focus on near-infrared fluorescent dyes and simultaneous detection of multiple analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, bacterial infections have become a major public health problem. The global burden caused by common bacterial pathogens not only led to serious economic losses but also threatened human health and interfered with normal life. Infection is undoubtedly an urgent global public health priority. In 2019, 7.7 million of the 13.7 million deaths from infections were linked to bacterial pathogens. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) alone was associated with more than 1 million deaths (Ikuta et al., 2022). Antimicrobials play an essential role in treating infection, but the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria puts their effectiveness at risk (Chen et al., 2017). According to a report by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), 4.95 million deaths were associated with bacterial antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in 2019, and it is estimated that the number could reach 10 million per year by 2050 United Nations Environment Programme (2023). To cope with this situation, effective detection and treatment strategies are critical to prevent and alleviate bacterial infections (Deusenbery et al., 2021). Prevention is the core of averting bacterial infections, emphasizing the urgent priority of rapid and sensitive bacterial detection.

Traditional detection methods have advantages, but they have several defects that fail to meet the needs of rapid and sensitive detection. The plate counting method has become the gold standard because of its reliability, but it is usually time-consuming due to the complex pretreatment process and long incubation time (Wang et al., 2021). Nucleic acid-based molecular biological detection methods are also widely used, such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR), real-time quantitative PCR (RT-PCR), and loop-mediated isothermal amplification method (LAMP) (De Falco et al., 2022; De Felice et al., 2023; Wang and Salazar, 2016), which have high sensitivity and relatively short detection time, but they are susceptible to the interference caused by external factors. More importantly, complicated steps and high specialization are also not conducive to the rapid on-site detection of bacteria. Immunoassays, such as enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA), have excellent specificity and sensitivity, which are attributed to the specific interaction between antibodies and antigens. However, the complex antibody preparation process, poor inter-batch repeatability, high cost, and poor stability restrict the application of immunoassay to some extent (Kim and Kim, 2021; Mi et al., 2022). Therefore, it is urgent to establish a facile, rapid, and sensitive method for bacterial detection.

Detection methods based on fluorescence sensing are broadly applied in multifarious fields on account of their simplicity, high sensitivity and the ability to rapid and real-time testing. The fluorescence sensing tactics for detecting target objects involve familiar design mechanisms, such as intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT), and FRET (Tarai et al., 2021). In particular, FRET-based fluorescence sensors have extensive applicability and exhibit good potential for bacterial detection, possessing the characteristics of high signal-to-noise ratio, high sensitivity, and less susceptibility to interference. Therefore, FRET-based sensors are a promising tool to meet the need for rapid and sensitive detection.

As far as we know, most current reviews of FRET summarize and elaborate on certain applications, such as applications in biological imaging and sensors for metal ions, small molecules, nucleic acid, and protein (Shen et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2019). Some reviews take materials as the entry point (Sapsford et al., 2006), analyze strategies, and introduce applications. There are already FRET-based applications for quantum dots (Stanisavljevic et al., 2015), functionalized up-conversion nanoparticles (Ansari et al., 2021), graphene and graphene-like two-denominational materials (Tian et al., 2017), and so on. However, comprehensive reviews describing the application of FRET to bacterial detection are still relatively rare (Bhupathi et al., 2023).

This review focuses on the development of FRET-based fluorescence assays for bacterial detection, allowing for their great potential and significance. In accordance with the previous literature about FRET, we summarize the principles of FRET from the perspective of prerequisites and influencing factors for process occurrence. Considering that the construction of FRET-based bacterial detection methods is not only related to the donor-acceptor but also inseparable from the role of recognition molecules. Thus, we discuss the commonly used recognition molecules and further introduce three frequent construction strategies. Many materials have been applied to FRET components due to their excellent properties, such as quantum dots, carbon dots, and up-conversion nanoparticles (Fig. 1 ). Based on the strategies and materials, relevant applications are presented. Moreover, some restrictions of FRET fluorescence sensors as well as corresponding improvement measures are discussed. Therefore, this review aims to clarify FRET-based assays in the rapid and sensitive detection of bacteria, expecting to provide a theoretical basis for the construction of methods and help readers gain insight into the great potential for the application of FRET.

FRET is a process in which electronic excitation energy is transferred between appropriate donor-acceptor pairs. Upon photoexcitation, the donor absorbs a photon of a certain frequency to an excited state. The donor in the excited state transfers energy to the adjacent acceptor in the ground state in a non-radiative manner through the long-range dipole–dipole interactions, leading to a fluorescence decrease in the donor (Fig. 2 ) (Wu et al., 2020). If the donor and acceptor are both fluorophores, the energy is absorbed by the acceptor and further excited to produce fluorescence (Shen et al., 2022).

To design an effective FRET platform, it is pre-requisite to satisfy the following principles: (1) The spectra of the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption have adequate overlap. (2) The distance between the donor and acceptor is typically 1–10 nm, which illustrates the distance dependence of the FRET process. (3) The dipole moment orientation of the donor-acceptor pair should be arranged in a suitable way.

In 1948, Theodor Förster proposed an equation that reveals the energy transfer efficiency between the single pair of donor-acceptor (Wu et al., 2020). As shown in eq (1) (Yuan et al., 2013), FRET efficiency (E) is inversely proportional to the sixth power of the distance (R) between the donor and acceptor, which demonstrates that FRET is quite relevant to the distance. When the transfer efficiency E = 50%, the distance of donor to acceptor is expressed as the Förster distance (R 0), which can be calculated through eq (2).
(1)
E = R 0 6/(R 0 6+R 6)
(2)
R 0 = 0.211[κ 2 n −4 Q D J(λ)]1/6 (in Å)

The factor κ 2 refers to the transition dipole orientation of the donor and acceptor with a value range from 0 to 4. Based on experience, κ 2 is generally taken as 2/3 in almost all biological forms (Sapsford et al., 2006). Where n is the refractive index, Q D is the quantum yield of the donor without the presence of the acceptor, and J(λ) is the overlap integral, which expresses the extent of spectral overlap between the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption. As shown in eq (3), the value of J(λ) can be attained through the integral computation.
(3)
J
( λ )
=
∫ 0 ∞
I D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ

The factor I D(λ) is the standard fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor, ε A(λ) is the acceptor molar absorption coefficient, and λ is the wavelength. In practice, we usually use the change of the maximum fluorescence intensity or fluorescence lifetime instead of the equations mentioned above when calculating energy transfer efficiency (Shen et al., 2022). As shown in eq (4) and eq (5), which are much easier to measure and calculate.
(4)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
(5)
E = 1 −
τ

F and F 0 are the maximum fluorescence intensity of the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor, respectively. τ and τ 0 are the fluorescent lifetime of the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor, respectively.

As can be seen from the above equations, factors such as J(λ) and Q D should be considered in the selection of the donor and acceptor. In addition, λ and ε A(λ) also be considered, which affects E by changing J(λ). The above formulas also provide a better understanding of the prerequisites required to build a FRET platform.

To achieve bacterial detection based on the FRET process, in addition to efficient and feasible FRET pairs, recognition molecules are also necessary for the connection between the donor and acceptor and for binding to the target object. The detection objects are not limited to the bacteria themselves but can also be bacteria-specific proteins or specific genes.

Bacterial detection is crucial for food analysis, environmental monitoring, and clinical diagnosis (Ahmed et al., 2014). The detection of bacteria using FRET is mainly achieved through the interaction between recognition molecules and bacteria. When detecting bacteria in real samples, a number of pre-treatment processes can be used to reduce the interference of impurities, such as magnetic separation (Xiao et al., 2022).

By using specific genes and proteins as direct detection targets, specific detection of bacteria can be achieved. Moreover, it is also widely used in the detection of drug resistance genes, bacterial identification, and other fields (Guo et al., 2017; Ha et al., 2021). However, in vitro detection of genes and proteins requires pre-treatment, including lysis of cells followed by extraction of the desired genes and proteins. FRET-based gene and protein detection usually requires specific recognition molecules. In addition, FRET sensors are very powerful for in vivo detection because they provide real-time, in-situ detection (Algar et al., 2019).

Appropriate recognition molecules have high affinity and selectivity for target bacteria. Under different strategies, recognition elements play different roles. Some of the commonly used recognition molecules are briefly described below, which can be divided into two groups based on the presence or absence of specificity.

Antibodies are commonly used biorecognition molecules that specifically recognize antigens through immune reaction. (Li et al., 2023; Yan et al., 2022). Antibodies have four chains assembled in a Y-shaped form, containing two identical structural domains for antigen recognition (Fab fragments) and two identical structural domains with effector functions (Fc fragments). The Fab fragments determine the specificity and affinity of antibodies. Bacterial antibodies primarily target components of the bacterial cell wall, such as surface proteins, lipopolysaccharides, and teichoic acid (Yu et al., 2023). Antibodies can also target bacterial toxins (Motley et al., 2019). Meanwhile, antibodies can be easily modified on the donor or acceptor by covalent coupling or streptavidin-biotin systems. (Xiao et al., 2022). Specific binding of antibodies and secondary antibodies can also be used to design FRET experiments. Due to their outstanding specificity and easy modification, antibodies are utilized for identification. However, the use of antibodies is limited by their high cost and poor reproducibility (Pehlivan et al., 2019).

Aptamers, single-stranded DNA or RNA, not only bind specifically to the target, but also are easily modified, stable, and inexpensive (Chen and Yang, 2015). Aptamers are obtained by an in vitro screening technique known as Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential enrichment (SELEX) (Pehlivan et al., 2019). Aptamers can recognize a wide range of target analytes such as proteins (Tran et al., 2013), heavy metals (Li et al., 2019a), bacteria (Bahari et al., 2021), nucleic acid (Shi et al., 2015a), etc. The end of the aptamer sequence can be labeled with various groups or molecules for chemical modification, such as amino groups, carboxyl groups, fluorophores, etc. (Xiao et al., 2022), which are easier to modify than antibodies. The modified chemical groups can be further linked to the nanomaterials. It is these characteristics that make aptamers widely used. By changing the aptamer, the designed sensor can be applied to the detection of other bacteria. The donor-acceptor pair's binding is often designed based on the nature of aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA) sequence hybridization (Jin et al., 2017). Furthermore, aptamer and single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) bind to the donor or acceptor through π-π stacking interaction, van der Waals forces, etc (Liang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2021).

Common non-specific recognition molecules such as antibiotics, lectins, and positively charged polymers have the ability to recognize bacteria in a broad spectrum. Antibiotics are able to recognize a class of bacteria by binding to special structures on their surface. For example, vancomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic that recognizes and binds to the D-Ala-D-Ala molecules on the surface of Gram-positive bacteria forming five hydrogen bonds (Rashid et al., 2021). Lectins are capable of being used as recognition molecules because they bind to carbohydrates on the bacterial surface such as peptidoglycans (Gamella et al., 2009). Such as Concanavalin A (Con A) binds to bacteria by binding to mannose residues (Dechtrirat et al., 2014). From the negatively charged nature of bacteria, positively charged polymers are adsorbed on the surface of bacteria through electrostatic interaction (Xiao et al., 2022). In addition, many other substances recognize bacteria by binding sites or other mechanisms, such as boric acid is covalently bound to the cis-o-dihydroxy structure contained in the bacterial surface saccharides (Wang et al., 2022b).

These recognition molecules are an indispensable part of bacterial detection. The different binding mechanisms make their properties different in some aspects. Since recognition molecules play a key role in FRET strategies, a combination of factors should be considered when selecting an appropriate recognition molecule.

The general strategy for constructing FRET detection platforms requires suitable donor-acceptor pairs and recognition molecules. After adding the target, the FRET process is turned on or off, leading to a change in the fluorescence signal that determines the presence or absence of the target. Similarly, the keys to the strategies for detecting bacteria based on FRET are the donor-acceptor pairs and the binding of recognition molecules. To date, there are mainly three common strategies that have been used for FRET-based bacterial detection, which depends on the different ways of donor-acceptor attachment (Fig. 3 ). By presenting the construction strategies, we hope to provide a general introduction to the design of the FRET process in bacterial detection, make them easier for readers to understand, and get enlightenment from them.

The first strategy is Single-stranded linking energy transferring. The donor linked with recognition molecules can attach to the acceptor in the absence of the target object, making FRET happen. When the target is added, the donor and acceptor segregate due to the stronger affinity of recognition molecules to the target (Wang et al., 2020). In this strategy, the donor is usually brought closer to the acceptor by the role of the recognition molecules. For example, aptamers can be adsorbed on graphene oxide (GO) through π-π stacking interactions. Therefore, the aptamer-modified donor material can be brought closer to GO to enable FRET to occur (Dehghani et al., 2020). However, in some cases, the donor is directly bound to the acceptor by some force, such as electrostatic interaction (Pebdeni et al., 2020).

The second strategy involves two recognition molecules that typically are aptamer and its cDNA sequence, which is Double-stranded hybridization energy transferring (Jin et al., 2017). The donor and acceptor are separately linked to two molecules, one with the ability to specifically recognize the target, such as aptamer, and the other molecule is usually its complementary strand. And the donor and acceptor can be brought closer through the interaction between these two molecules, leading to the occurrence of FRET. In the presence of target bacteria, the molecule with the ability to specifically recognize the target binds preferentially to bacteria and thereby separates the donor and acceptor, resulting in the alternation of fluorescence. Similarly, the interaction between antibodies and secondary antibodies is also used in this strategy (Wang et al., 2015).

The last strategy refers to two recognition molecules and the FRET process depends on the detection target, that is, Target energy transferring. The donor and acceptor are separately linked to recognition molecules that both can recognize bacteria specifically or nonspecifically, forming the sandwich structure and causing FRET to occur. The process cannot occur under conditions in which targets are not present. In this strategy, many specific or non-specific recognition molecules can be used to construct a dual-recognition detection platform (Yu et al., 2017). In addition, some platforms utilize complementary sequences of target genes to design the capture probe and reporter probe, which are then linked to the donor and acceptor for the construction of the FRET process, respectively (Shi et al., 2015a).

These three construction strategies depend on different ways of connection between the donor and acceptor. The first strategy involves one type of recognition molecule that has a stronger affinity for the target, thus showing the FRET process from on to off as the target is added. The second strategy involves two molecules, but only one of them has a recognition effect on the target, and the FRET process usually exhibits from on to off upon the addition of the target. The third strategy refers to two recognition molecules and relies on the dual recognition effect on the target to construct the FRET process, showing FRET from off to on after adding the target.

Organic fluorescent dyes, such as cyanine (Sun et al., 2016), fluorescein (Sapsford et al., 2006), boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) (Lu et al., 2014), rhodamine (Wang et al., 2019), and so on, play an important role in FRET-based bacterial detection. These fluorescent dyes commonly contain benzene or heterocycles with conjugated double bonds and share some typical features including compact arrangement and small Stokes shift (Sapsford et al., 2006). The small Stokes shift is adverse for the application of organic dyes, which may lead to problems such as self-quenching and detection errors due to the excitation backscattering effects (Fan et al., 2013). Especially for FRET applications, this leads to direct excitation of the acceptor, making the analysis more complex. However, organic dyes have the advantages of low cost, commercial availability, and easy modification, and their optical properties have been widely reported in the literature, so they are still widely used (Liang et al., 2023).

Classical cyanine dyes consist of a structure in which a positively charged nitrogen center is connected to another nitrogen center by a conjugated chain composed of an odd number of carbon atoms (Sun et al., 2016). The structural features result in a high extinction coefficient for the cyanine dyes. The absorption and emission spectra of the cyanine dyes can be changed with the length of the polymethine chain so that cyanine dyes can cover almost all commonly used fluorescent bands. However, the classical cyanine dyes are subject to limitations in practical application, including poor photostability and undesirable aggregation in aqueous solution. However, these limitations can be alleviated to some extent by modification. For example, water solubility is improved by introducing sulfonation groups. The incorporation of dyes into silica nanoparticles has been shown to be a good way to improve photostability. The introduction of other groups such as alkylamino groups induces excited-state ICT to increase Stokes shift (Peng et al., 2005). Therefore, a series of cyanine dyes-based sensors have been applied in samples due to the better performance of dyes-based sensors obtained by modification.

Fluorescein dyes are an important class of oxanthrene fluorescent dyes, which are widely used in the design of molecular probes and detection. The presence of the phenyl hydroxyl group and the carboxyl group makes them more water-soluble. By replacing the active hydrogen on fluorescein, various fluorescein derivatives can be generated. Introduction of isothiocyanate and carboxyl group results in the formation of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and carboxyfluorescein (FAM), respectively. Due to their high extinction coefficient and good water solubility, fluorescein dyes have attracted wide attention. But at the same time, the undesirable properties of fluorescein dyes constrain their applications. For instance, fluorescein dyes are sensitive to light, which makes them susceptible to photobleaching during strong light detection. And they are pH-sensitive, which limits their application in the detection, but this may also provide an advantage in certain application scenarios, such as monitoring intracellular pH (Sapsford et al., 2006).

Rhodamine dyes have moderate hydrophilicity, high absorption coefficient, and high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), which makes them widely used as fluorescent markers in biological imaging, fluorescence analysis, and other fields. In addition to their excellent optical properties, the special structure containing three benzene rings is also the reason for their popularity. The equilibrium of ring conjugation is controlled by the balance between spironolactone and ring opening (Wang et al., 2019). The process of ring opening through chelation and reaction can allow their fluorescence to be greatly enhanced. This property can be exploited for the detection of analytes such as metal ions. However, rhodamine dyes also have the disadvantage of low signal-to-noise ratio. The properties are optimized by extending their structure and replacing oxygen atoms with other elements.

Boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) dyes, a structural analog of porphyrin, have been widely used in fluorescence imaging and sensor applications due to their easy synthesis, structural versatility, and excellent spectral properties. They have the characteristics of high molar absorption coefficient, good photostability, and good solubility. However, the spectra of classical BODIPY dyes are limited to the region of 470–530 nm. The redshift of the spectrum can be achieved by the formation of aza-BODIPY or by the introduction of groups such as aryl and ethynyl (Lu et al., 2014).

It is obvious that FRET based on organic dyes is widely used due to their easy modification, non-specific binding, and other advantages. A method of targeting ssDNA that can simultaneously detect multiple foodborne bacteria was proposed by Xing and coworkers (Xing et al., 2023). Based on a smartphone microfluidic biosensing platform, the approach utilized FAM-labeled ctDNA that could pair with target ssDNA as the donor and GO as the acceptor (Fig. 4 a). In the absence of target ssDNA, the fluorescence of FAM was effectively quenched by GO through the FRET process. However, the green fluorescence recovered after the addition of ssDNA. Combined with the use of microfluidic chips, this method was simple and efficient, which could simultaneously detect four ssDNA within 5 min with a low detection limit. It also enabled further high-throughput analysis of ssDNA in milk and water samples. There are also aptamer sensors, which usually use aptamers labeled with fluorescent dyes as donors. In like manner, Gupta and coworkers established an aptamer sensor for Salmonella Typhimurium (S. typhimurium), using FAM-labeled aptamer and GO as the FRET pair for detection (Gupta et al., 2023).

For the fluorescent dyes-based FRET process, DNA scaffolds are popular and readily labeled with dyes. Wijesinghe and coworkers reported a FRET assay for iron-regulated surface determinant protein A (IsdA), a key surface protein of S. aureus that plays an important role in bacterial dispersal (Wijesinghe et al., 2023). The construct was assembled from five DNA strands, two of which were labeled with cyanine 3 (Cy3) and cyanine 5 (Cy5), one top strand, the aptamer, and one blocker strand (B1). B1 was partially complementary to the Cy5-tagged strand and partially complementary to the aptamer. As shown in Fig. 4b, in the absence of the target protein, the fluorophores were far away and presented a low-medium FRET state. Upon addition of the target protein and the H1 strand fully complementary to B1, the aptamer bound with the protein and then separated from the construct, which in turn dissociated B1 from the structure by a process called toehold-mediated strand displacement (TMSD). As a consequence, this allowed Cy3 and Cy5 on the opposite arm of the structure to aggregate and trigger high FRET. The proposed method proved the detection of IsdA downed to picomolar level and the dynamic range extended to ∼40 nM. The study could further detect the whole isolated target as well as the target between mixed bacteria in the substrate of food materials. This approach was not exactly the same as the overview of the Double-strand hybridization energy transferring strategy, but there were similarities in that it was the hybridization of the two chains that brought the donor-acceptor in close proximity and the FRET process occurred.

The use of DNA scaffolds has important significance for the application of dyes-based FRET. And the dyes can be labeled not only on DNA but also on other nonspecific markers. For example, the FRET platform constructed by Wang and coworkers was based on the Target energy transferring, using FITC-labeled lysozyme as the donor and polyethylenimine-modified gold nanoparticles (PEI-AuNPs) as the acceptor (Fig. 4c) (Wang et al., 2022a). The donor bound to the bacteria through recognition of peptidoglycan and electrostatic interaction, while the acceptor bound to the bacteria through electrostatic interaction. Therefore, the addition of bacteria resulted in fluorescence quenching with reduced-distance. This method could detect a variety of bacteria with a broad spectrum. Under the experimental conditions, the results were not disturbed by impurities in the sample after ten times dilution.

In summary, there is a wide variety of organic fluorescent dyes with emission spanning the UV to IR spectrum, which meets the design requirements of most FRET processes. The function of DNA scaffolds makes their applications simple and extensive. Moreover, organic dyes will remain an indispensable part of FRET applications by improving the defects of existing dyes and developing near-infrared dyes.

Quantum dots (QDs), also known as semiconductor quantum dots, are zero-dimensional nanocrystal materials that mainly consist of the element of II-VI or III-V group (Pleskova et al., 2018). The motion of excitons is limited in all three dimensions, and the continuous energy band structure is transformed into a discrete energy level structure with molecular properties, allowing QDs to exhibit properties intermediate between those of large semiconductors and discrete molecules (Du et al., 2021). Meanwhile, as the particle size enters the nanometer order, the size limitation will give rise to the quantum limitation. As a result, QDs have high fluorescence intensity, long fluorescence life, and large Stokes shift.

The size of QDs is usually in the range of 2–10 nm. The optical absorption range can be regulated by adjusting the size so that the QDs have a large and continuous absorption spectrum (Bajorowicz et al., 2018). In comparison to organic dyes, QDs are 10–20 times brighter (Gidwani et al., 2021). Meanwhile, they have stable fluorescence and sufficient intensity to be detected. The fluorescence lifetime can reach tens of nanoseconds, which is longer than the few nanoseconds of fluorescent dyes. The large Stokes shift makes them less disturbed by the background and has higher accuracy for detection.

In terms of structure, QDs mainly include core-type QDs, core-shell QDs, and alloyed QDs (Gidwani et al., 2021). The core-type QDs are usually composed of CdSe, CdTe and InAs. They are easy to synthesize, but there are some defects in practical application, such as toxic effect, blinking effect, and photobleaching effect. To overcome the disadvantages of the core-type QDs, the core-shell QDs are designed. The addition of the shell can reduce the toxicity, improve the water solubility, provide sites for ligand binding, and improves optical properties. Common core-shell structures use ZnS as a shell. The approach of adjusting the properties of QDs by changing their size can cause problems in many size-constrained applications. In contrast, alloy QDs provide a way to tune properties without changing size, but to tune the optical and electronic properties by simply changing the composition and internal structure (Bailey and Nie, 2003).

The appropriate preparation method is selected according to the material used, desired size, QY, and application scenario (Gidwani et al., 2021). High-temperature synthesis is beneficial to the synthesis of QDs with high QY and stable photoluminescence (PL), which is related to the choice of ligands (Zhong et al., 2003). γ-irradiation synthesis can be used in the preparation of QDs by irradiating an aqueous solution of the precursor to obtain colloidal products (Kang et al., 2008). Microwave-assisted method is fast, simple, environmentally friendly, and has uniform nucleation, which can be used in synthesizing CdS QDs, CdSe-based QDs, and so on, but it limits the size of the reaction sample (Singh et al., 2019). The sol-gel method involves dispersing the precursor material in a solvent, forming a sol through hydrolysis or condensation reactions, and then further generating a gel with a certain spatial structure. The generated gel is then subjected to steps such as heat treatment to obtain the desired material. The method has the advantages of high production efficiency and controllable particle size (Liu et al., 2012). Core-shell structure is obtained by the formation of metalcore and epitaxial growth on the surface, which can prepare QDs with stable optical properties. They are beneficial for bioconjugation and more conducive to application.

QDs are often used in FRET-based fluorescence sensors due to their excellent optical properties. Liang and coworkers designed a FRET sensor based on genetic detection to detect the Mycobacterium tuberculosis (M. tuberculosis) IS6110 gene fragment, using QDs-labeled ssDNA probe as the energy donor and a two-dimensional metal-organic framework Cu-TCPP as the energy acceptor (Fig. 5 a) (Liang et al., 2021). In the absence of the target, the ssDNA was adsorbed on the surface of Cu-TCPP through π-π stacking interaction, thereby allowing FRET to occur and resulting in fluorescence quenching. Upon addition of the target gene, the ssDNA hybridized with the target to form a double-stranded DNA (dsDNA), thus restoring fluorescence. And the sensor could be successfully applied to the detection of clinical sputum samples.

In addition to being the donor, QDs can also act as the acceptor. Wang and coworkers constructed a FRET system based on two-color QDs acting as both the donor and acceptor and combining immune response for the detection of Salmonella enteritidis (S. Enteritidis) (Wang et al., 2015). The green-emitting QDs were coupled to the anti-S. Enteritidis antibodies (Ab1) through carbodiimide bond as the energy donor and the orange-emitting QDs were coupled to the secondary antibody for rabbit anti-S. Enteritidis antibodies (Ab2) as the energy acceptor (Fig. 5b). Due to the affinity of the antibody and secondary antibody, the proximity between the two QDs triggered the FRET process, leading to the quenching of the green QDs and the fluorescence enhancement of the orange QDs. In the presence of the target bacteria, the Ab1 was bound to the bacteria by immune recognition, blocking the connection of the donor and acceptor so that the FRET process could not occur.

Apart from the systems based on the strategies described above, there is the platform based on the Target energy transferring. The system used a specific aptamer-modified QDs as the energy donor and Con A-modified AuNPs as the energy acceptor to act as a dual recognition sensor (Fig. 5c) (Zhang et al., 2023). Upon interaction with target bacteria, both the donor and acceptor bound to the target, bringing them in close proximity and causing FRET to occur. Based on this strategy, a sandwich-form FRET-based biosensor was developed to detect the conserved genomic region of the 6-kDa early secretory antigen target 6 (ESAT-6) for M. tuberculosis (Shojaei et al., 2014). QDs and AuNPs were linked to two specific nucleotides targeting DNA, respectively, and after adding the complementary target DNA, the FRET process occurred and the fluorescence was quenched.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, whose structure consists of sp2/sp3 carbon and oxygen/nitrogen-based groups or polymeric aggregations (Zhu et al., 2015). The carbon core-based structure endows CDs with good photostability, lower toxicity, and better biocompatibility compared with organic dyes and traditional semiconductor QDs. There are some superior properties under the influence of sp2 conjugated structure, such as high specific surface area and robust surface grafting through π-π stacking (Yuan et al., 2016). Besides, the surface modification of oxygen/nitrogen-based groups or polymeric aggregations improves the physical properties, which makes them have better solubility and more binding sites. Meanwhile, it enhances the optical properties with different colors and higher QY.

CDs are mainly divided into three types: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs). GQDs usually have graphene lattices that are the same as one or a few layers of graphene, as they are typically fabricated through graphite-based materials (Li et al., 2019b). The conversion from two-dimensional to zero-dimensional materials leads to quantum confinement and edge effects, which in turn contribute to the distinctive properties of GQDs. At the same time, the non-zero bandgap enables GQDs to obtain higher energy spectra (Tian et al., 2018). CNDs broadly include quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which are divided into carbon nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) according to the absence or presence of crystal lattice (Song et al., 2014b; Zhu et al., 2015). CQDs with distinct crystal lattices exhibit quantum confinement effect of size, making it possible to tune the PL wavelength by adjusting the size (Xia et al., 2019). PDs are a class of CDs with polymer characteristics that can be formed by preparation from linear polymers or monomers or by self-assembly of carbon core and linked polymer chains (Zhu et al., 2015). PDs not only possess the optical properties of CDs but also possess the properties of polymer. The polymer chain facilitates the passivation and stabilization of the luminescence center (Song et al., 2014b).

There are a lot of synthetic methods due to the diversity of CDs, but the two general strategies are top-down and bottom-up (Zhu et al., 2015). In the top-down synthesis process, the bulk carbon materials are destroyed by physical or chemical methods to generate small-size CDs. These precursor carbon materials possess sp2 carbon structure but lack the efficient bandgap to emit fluorescence, including graphite-based materials, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and so on (Yuan et al., 2016). In this strategy, arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation are commonly used to prepare CDs (Shen et al., 2020). The top-down synthesis methods are usually complex and difficult to control and require harsh conditions, resulting in relatively low yield and QY, which are not suitable for subsequent applications (Ji et al., 2020). The bottom-up approaches make use of small carbon-containing molecules or polymers to fabricate CDs. These precursors include small organic molecules such as citric acid, phenylenediamines, polyols, and amino acids; polymers such as polyethylene glycol; and natural products such as carbohydrates and biomass (Ji et al., 2020). Among them, biomass can be divided into three categories: protein products, organisms, and waste materials, which have received extensive attention due to their concept of green synthesis and sustainability (Rasheed, 2023). The preparation of CDs from biomass is low-cost, easy, and safe, which is beneficial for the practical application of CDs. There are many approaches for carrying out the bottom-up strategy, such as hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, carbonization, and so on. CDs with excellent size, shape, and properties can be prepared on demand by elaborating suitable precursors. Alternatively, the QY of the CDs can be improved by surface functionalization and passivation (Zhu et al., 2015).

CDs share similar absorption and fluorescence properties in spite of the diversity of the structure. The typical absorption of CDs is characterized by strong absorption in the UV region (230–320 nm), and then the intensity gradually decreases to the visible region, which is attributed to the π→π* transition for the CC bond and the n→π* transition for the CO bond. The tunable PL properties make CDs so remarkable. The PL colors of CDs are mostly blue and green, but full coverage from blue to red light can be achieved by different methods (Yuan et al., 2016). The emission peaks are usually broad with large Stokes shifts. Besides, the position of their emission peaks varies with the excitation wavelength, also known as excitation-dependent behavior. Photostability is also the key to much attention paid to CDs, which refers to no blinking and meaningful reductions in PL intensity after continuous exposure to excitation (Zhu et al., 2015).

CDs are promising materials for detecting bacteria owing to their excellent optical properties and lower toxicity in comparison to organic dyes. Dehghani and coworkers developed a fluorescent immunosensor based on GQDs and GO for the detection of Campylobacter jejuni (C. jejuni) in food samples (Dehghani et al., 2020). In the absence of target bacteria, GQDs conjugated with polyclonal antibodies were quenched by π-π stacking interaction with GO. Upon addition of target bacteria, the antibodies bound specifically to the bacteria and the fluorescence recovered. In addition, an aptamer sensor based on CQDs and AuNPs was designed to detect S. aureus (Pebdeni et al., 2020). First, CQDs were synthesized from olive leaves by hydrothermal method. Since the maximum emission wavelength of CQDs at 430 nm overlapped with the absorption of AuNPs, FRET process with CQDs as the donor and AuNPs as the acceptor could occur. Through the electrostatic interaction between the amino groups on the surface of CQDs and the citrate-stabilized AuNPs, FRET occurred and the fluorescence quenched. However, after the addition of target bacteria, AuNPs were separated from the CQDs due to the attachment between the aptamer and target, causing the fluorescence to recover. This approach linked the donor-acceptor through electrostatic interaction rather than by the action of the aptamer.

Based on the second strategy, Cui and coworkers established a magnetic fluorescence sensor for the isolation and detection of S. aureus (Cui et al., 2019). Multicolor CDs with high fluorescence stability, long fluorescence lifetime (6.37 ns), and high absolute QY were synthesized by hydrothermal method. CDs modified by cDNA and Fe3O4 modified by aptamer participated in the FRET process as the donor and acceptor, respectively (Fig. 6 a). Aptamer and cDNA hybridized and compressed the distance, causing FRET. The presence of the target bacteria led to the disintegration of the donor and acceptor and recovery of fluorescence. At the same time, the captured bacteria could be separated by a magnetic field for further count and comparison. The method could be completed within 30 min. Meanwhile, the platform could be applied to the detection of other bacteria by changing the aptamer.

Up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can convert lower energy excitation light into higher energy emission light, which is a nonlinear optical process by absorbing two or more low-energy photons and then emitting as a single high-energy photon (Xu et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2015). The up-conversion luminescence is different from the luminescence process of the above fluorescent materials, which follows the anti-Stokes luminescence law. Although the up-conversion effect was discovered in the 1960s, it was restricted to bulk glass or crystalline materials. With the development of nanotechnology, UCNPs are fabricated and moved into the spotlight as an efficient platform for detection. Compared with organic dyes, QDs, and CDs, UCNPs manifest superior properties, such as large Stokes shift, sharp emission bandwidth, and long excited state lifetime. The self-fluorescent background is almost zero, which can improve the signal-to-noise ratio. Moreover, because the excitation is located in the near-infrared region, they have the characteristics of high resistance to photo-bleaching and allowing deep tissue to penetrate (Li et al., 2015).

UCNPs are usually composed of sensitizer ions and activator ions doped into the matrix in an ordered manner. Sensitizers capture the energy of excitation light and can transfer it to other ions, of which Yb3+ is the most widely used sensitizer ion. Activators accept energy transition to high energy levels and finally carry out fluorescence emission, which is usually Er3+, Tm3+, and Ho3+ ions. The main matrix provides the crystal field required for the activator to emit light. NaYF4 is a commonly used up-conversion matrix material that can provide high up-conversion efficiency (Gu et al., 2013). It is the unique electronic structure of the rare earth ions in the crystal that enables the UCNPs to acquire particular optical characteristics (Zheng et al., 2015). The up-conversion luminescence system composed of host substrate and lanthanide doping can be divided into three main luminescence mechanisms: excited state absorption (ESA), energy transfer upconversion (ETU), and photon avalanche (PA) (Zhou et al., 2015). All three mechanisms contain the sequential absorption of two or more photons (Haase and Schäfer, 2011).

The particle size, morphology, and optical properties can be modulated by different synthesis methods and modifications. At present, the preparation methods of UCNPs have been relatively mature, and the commonly used methods include hydrothermal method, solvothermal method, thermal decomposition method, and co-precipitation method (Lin et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2019). Thermal decomposition and solvothermal methods are two approaches for the preparation of high-quality UCNPs. The thermal decomposition method can obtain good particle size and morphology in a short time, but it has high requirements for equipment and is easy to produce harmful gases. The solvothermal method is widely used because of its simple operation, good crystallinity, and good monodispersity of UCNPs, but it requires high temperature, long reaction time, and closed environment. The hydrothermal method has the same defects, whereas UCNPs are synthesized with better hydrophilicity and less pollution to the environment. The coprecipitation method has simple conditions, but it is difficult to control the crystal phase and particle size. According to the application scenario, the appropriate method is selected to prepare UCNPs. Furthermore, superior UCNPs can be achieved through additional surface modification. And the formation of a core-shell structure is a commonly used method.

Due to their excellent optical properties and easily modified sites, UCNPs are the ideal energy donors. Wang and coworkers developed a FRET platform based on layered tungsten disulfide (WS2) nanosheets and aptamer-functionalized UCNPs for the detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli) (Fig. 6b) (Wang et al., 2020). The WS2 nanosheets as the acceptor have the characteristics of high specific surface area and high dispersibility in water. Similar to organic graphene, they can spontaneously adsorb ssDNA under the van der Waals interaction, and show strong absorption and high quenching efficiency in the near-infrared region. Therefore, aptamer-modified UCNPs could be adsorbed on the WS2 nanosheets, and the absorption of WS2 nanosheets (200–800 nm) overlapped with the emission spectrum of UCNPs, allowing the FRET process to occur and fluorescence quenching. However, after the addition of bacteria, the aptamer preferentially bound to E. coli, causing the dissociation of UCNPs from the WS2 nanosheets and the recovery of fluorescence. Thus, WS2 nanosheets and aptamer-functionalized UCNPs could serve as the FRET pair for bacterial detection.

Jin and coworkers developed an aptamer sensor based on UCNPs and AuNPs. AuNPs bound with bacteria-targeted aptamers were selected as the acceptor, while UCNPs functionalized with cDNA were selected as the donor (Fig. 6c) (Jin et al., 2017). UCNPs were synthesized by a thermal decomposition procedure with ligand exchange by Polyacrylic acid (PAA) modification to replace the original hydrophobic groups on the surface. Their emission peak was 540 nm, which overlapped with the absorption peak of AuNPs, indicating that FRET may occur. By complementary pairing of cDNA and aptamer, the distance was narrowed, followed by FRET and fluorescence quenching. Once the target bacteria were added, the aptamer bound preferentially to the target, resulting in donor-acceptor separation and fluorescence recovery. The distance at which the FRET process took place in this study was higher than the typical distance, probably due to surface plasmon resonances around AuNPs. This sensor could be applied for rapid and ultra-sensitive detection of E. coli in pool water and milk samples within 20 min.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are attractive nanomaterials due to their special optical and electrochemical properties. Often acting as quenchers in FRET systems, AuNPs have been demonstrated to possess many advantages. Firstly, one of the most important properties of AuNPs is localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), a phenomenon that exhibits a characteristic peak wavelength that is highly dependent on the shape and size. Because of this phenomenon, the quenching of fluorescence by AuNPs can be optimized by changing the shape and size to a wavelength close to the emission maximum of fluorescence donor. In addition, AuNPs have high quenching efficiency over a wide spectral range. For example, AuNPs with a diameter of 1.4 nm can quench fluorescence 100-fold higher than conventional organic quenchers (Ling and Huang, 2010). In the FRET using AuNPs as the acceptor, this process occurs at a distance as long as 70–100 nm, which is almost 10 times farther than the traditional FRET pairs. This is owing to the collective resonance oscillation caused by the dipole-surface effect of spherical AuNPs energy transfer, which involves the (distance)−4-dependent energy transfer efficiency (Shi et al., 2015b). More importantly, AuNPs have a high specific surface area, which can provide reactive regions for multiple donors as well as binding sites for modifications. It is especially easy to attach biological or organic ligands to the AuNPs surface through Au–S bonds.

Stable AuNPs can be prepared by chemical or physical pathways, which can be divided into top-down and bottom-up approaches. From top to bottom, AuNPs are obtained by crushing the bulk gold into nanoparticles, including chemical etching, pyrolysis, and lithography, etc. The top-down method has the advantages of being fast and simple, but it is difficult to control the shape, size, and harsh conditions. Moreover, considering that the LSPR peaks of AuNPs have size and shape dependence, this method is not conducive to the design of FRET. The bottom-up is formed by the self-assembly of the precursor, which involves the reduction of Au ions to Au and the assembly of Au atoms. The process consists of two steps of nucleation and subsequent growth. This assembly method can control the shape and size, but the toxic reagent is its main drawback. The most commonly used method is to synthesize AuNPs by reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4) in the presence of sodium citrate (Anh et al., 2022).

The ability to broadly overlap with the emission spectra of a wide variety of donors, the high quenching efficiency, and the ease of modification are all sufficient to make AuNPs ideal acceptors/quenchers. Using the FRET process between Rhodamine B (RB) and AuNPs, Srinivasan and coworkers constructed an ingenious method for the detection of S. typhimurium (Fig. 7 a) (Srinivasan et al., 2018). This method took advantage of the mechanism by which AuNPs induce aggregation in a high-salt environment, while at appropriate aptamer concentration, AuNPs could coil with aptamer to avoid NaCl-induced aggregation. After the addition of RB, RB was adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs by electrostatic adsorption, thereby FRET occurred and RB was quenched. Once S. typhimurium was added, the aptamer bound specifically to the bacteria. AuNPs lost the protection of the aptamer and were induced to aggregate by NaCl. Therefore, FRET could not occur and the quenched fluorescence was recovered. Instead of playing the role of connecting the donor-acceptor, the aptamer avoided the aggregation of AuNPs, and FRET occurred when the donor-acceptor was brought closer by electrostatic adsorption.

The application of AuNPs in the energy transfer strategy through double-stranded hybridization has been mentioned above (Jin et al., 2017). In addition, AuNPs are widely used in the Target energy transferring strategy due to their easy modification properties. Shi and coworkers developed a FRET biosensor based on GQDs and AuNPs to detect the mecA gene sequence of S. aureus (Shi et al., 2015a). The capture probe and reporter probe designed with the specific sequence of mecA were combined with GQDs and AuNPs as the donor and acceptor, respectively (Fig. 7b). Once the target oligonucleotide was added, the distance was closer through the hybridization of complementary oligonucleotides, and FRET occurred. In addition, it had good sequence selectivity. However, it should be noted that the short gene sequence of the whole genome of S. aureus was used as the target oligonucleotide in this study.

Yu and coworkers designed a FRET strategy based on AuNPs as the acceptor for the detection of S. aureus (Yu et al., 2017). Vancomycin-functionalized gold nanoclusters (Van-AuNCs) and aptamer-modified AuNPs acted as the energy donor and acceptor, respectively, allowing the FRET process to occur after the addition of the target. And the fluorescence signal varied with bacterial concentration. In the absence of target bacteria, Van-AuNCs retained their blue fluorescence. Similarly, Sena and coworkers established the platform for the detection of E. coli using azithromycin-functionalized Cerium oxide nanoparticles (Azm-CeO2NPs) and aptamer-modified AuNPs as the FRET pair (Ghayyem and Faridbod, 2022). CeO2NPs belong to rare earth oxides, which possess a blend of Ce4+ and Ce3+ cations and have good luminescence characteristics. Azm bound to the 50S ribosomal subunit of bacteria, so FRET occurred through the combination of Azm and aptamer with bacteria. This method has been successfully applied to the detection of E. coli in milk and whey samples.

Graphene oxide (GO), a member of the graphene family, has both the two-dimensional atomic layer of the original graphene to give it a hydrophobic structure and the oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxy) generated in the oxidation process at the edge and basal surface to give it a hydrophilic structure (Lee et al., 2016). In terms of its electronic structure, it is the coexistence of π-state sp2 carbon clusters and σ state sp3 C–O matrix, forming a strange heterogeneous structure (Tian et al., 2017). The peculiar structure gives GO excellent performance. GO has a broad absorption range from 200 nm to 800 nm, which can easily meet the requirements of overlapping with the emission spectrum of the donor. Meanwhile, compared to traditional FRET pairs, the FRET process with GO as the acceptor can occur at a longer distance, which is dependent on (distance)−4. The large surface-to-volume provides the superiority of having a lot of binding sites, allowing one acceptor to multiple donors simultaneously (Shi et al., 2015b). Besides, the groups at the edge and basal surface make it easier to implement covalent binding, while the abundant π-state electrons can link with DNA by π-π stacking interaction, making GO more suitable for use in the FRET platform (Gao et al., 2014). The superiority of high quenching efficiency, abundant functional groups, and good solubility are why GO has attracted wide attention. GO is usually prepared by Brodie, Staudenmaier, and Hummers methods or these methods with modification (Lee et al., 2016).

On account of the excellent properties of GO, a GO-coated nanopaper (GONAP) was developed for further application in antibody-bound QDs and GO as the FRET pair (Fig. 8 a) (Cheeveewattanagul et al., 2017). The coworkers used it to construct an immunosensing platform to detect E. coli. GONAP was formed by hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl group of the bacterial cellulose nanopaper (BC) and the hydroxyl group of GO. In the absence of target bacteria, CdSe@ZnS QDs were adsorbed to GONAP, enabling FRET to occur and leading to fluorescence quenching due to the proximity. Upon the addition of E. coli, the antigen-antibody-QDs complex was desorbed from GONAP by a series of force. Then, the antigen was adsorbed on the surface of GONAP again through electrostatic interaction, which increased the distance between the donor and acceptor, preventing FRET and restoring fluorescence. This method had good specificity and could also be applied to the detection of proteins. In like manner, aptamer-coupled UCNPs formed a FRET pair with GO for rapid detection of E. coli (Yao et al., 2021). At the same time, GO was equipped with Fe3O4, which had better water solubility. Through the π-π stacking interaction between the aptamer and GO, the donor and acceptor approached, allowing FRET to occur and quenching fluorescence. In the presence of target bacteria, aptamer-UCNPs recognized bacteria to desorb from GO@Fe3O4. After magnetic separation, the donor and acceptor were further separated to prevent FRET, which simplified the procedure.

Guo and coworkers developed a genetic assay targeting the Salmonella invA gene (Fig. 8b) (Guo et al., 2017). Using QDs and GO as the donor and acceptor, capture B and capture A were bound with the help of ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide/N-Hydroxy succinimide (EDC/NHS), respectively, which were derived from the positive strand of and complementary to the invA gene of Salmonella. To reduce the interference caused by nonspecific adsorption between the donor and acceptor, the GO-capture A was passivated using BSA. When invA oligonucleotide was added, it served as a bridge to connect the donor and acceptor, and FRET occurred at the near distance.

Black Hole Quenchers (BHQs), a kind of dark quench agent, are commonly used as the acceptor in FRET assays. With the structure of a polyaromatic-azo backbone, BHQs have no natural fluorescence, resulting in low background fluorescence and high signal-to-noise ratio. In addition, they exhibit a wide range of absorption spectra and high extinction coefficient. The frequently-used BHQs, such as BHQ-1, range from 480 to 580 nm, which has a maximum absorption wavelength of 534 nm. The absorption spectrum of BHQ-3 is close to the infrared region. BHQ-2 has an absorption wavelength of 550–650 nm, which can effectively quench FAM, Cy3, and Cy5 (Fang et al., 2022).

Because of their properties, BHQs are often used to label oligonucleotides as the acceptor to quench fluorescence. Song and coworkers devised an assay for the detection of S. enteritidis using carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and BHQ-1 as the donor and acceptor, which simultaneously incorporated signal amplification assisted by nicking enzyme (Fig. 8c) (Song et al., 2014a). The sensor was composed of ssDNA modified with carbon nanotube (CNT-DNA sequence) and molecular beacons (MB) constructed by CNPs and BHQ-1. The CNT-DNA sequence contained a complementary DNA sequence for 16S rRNA and a complementary DNA sequence that could recognize the nicking enzyme. After the addition of the target sequences, CNT-DNA unwound and bound to the target to remain more stable, while MB could hybridize with CNT-DNA to form double-stranded DNA (ds-DNA). Due to its cleavage site, the nicking enzyme played a role in cutting MB, making CNPs far away from BHQ-1 and unable to undergo FRET. In the absence of the target, FRET occurred when CNT-DNA was wrapped around the CNT due to the π-π stacking interaction between the bases and CNT, which prevented sequence release and MB cleavage.

Liu and coworkers constructed a FRET platform that used UCNPs modified by aptamer and BHQ-1 coupled with cDNA as the donor and acceptor, respectively (Fig. 8d) (Liu et al., 2022). The synthesized UCNPs had an emission spectrum range of 450–600 nm, which could well overlap with the BHQ-1 absorption spectrum. When aptamer hybridized to cDNA, the donor-acceptor distance was shortened and FRET occurred. After the addition of target, the donor-acceptor dissociated due to the preferential binding of the aptamer to bacteria, thereby fluorescence recovered.

Rapid and sensitive detection of bacteria is pre-requisite and pressing to defend public health. Recently, FRET-based fluorescence sensor has exhibited extensive potential and applicability for bacterial detection considering the characteristic of high signal-to-noise ratio and high sensitivity. Based on this situation, we formulated the development of FRET-based sensors for bacterial detection. We began with the essential principle and correlative factors of the FRET process. Considering the detection of bacteria, not only effective and feasible donor-acceptor pairs but also appropriate recognition molecules were required. Then, we introduced the recognition molecules and construction strategies. Note that the three strategies summarized cannot include all cases, and there are many improved methods based on the three strategies that use special properties of materials to design. In the last section, the donor-acceptor materials and applications according to the strategies mentioned above were discussed. Despite the bright development prospect of the FRET-based fluorescence platform in the field of bacterial detection, there are still some challenges and problems to be solved or improved.

Firstly, the characteristics of donor-acceptor pairs play a decisive role in the detection, which is related to the sensitivity of the detection and the stability of the detection signal. With the features of low background fluorescence, high fluorescence stability and intensity, long fluorescence life, and high quantum yield, the donors are generally perceived as the more suitable choices. Meanwhile, the acceptors with broad absorption and high quenching efficiency have more superiorities. In addition, good water solubility and low toxicity are equally important. At present, the above requirements are met to some extent by the emergence of nanomaterials, and suitable FRET pairs can be obtained by changing the synthesis methods and conditions.

Secondly, the significance of the recognition molecules is self-evident. Recognition elements are a bridge connecting bacteria to FRET pairs, which is also the basis of construction strategies. Common recognition molecules have their advantages as well as some drawbacks. For example, aptamers have good affinity and specificity, but also suffer from the disadvantage of being easily interfered with in complex matrices. Therefore, stable and economical recognition molecules still require considerable effort to be developed. The appropriate recognition molecules need to be considered in combination with the specific bacterial surface molecules and the surface modification of the donor and acceptor. The interference problem of complex matrices can also be improved by a simple and efficient pretreatment process.

Lastly, despite the rapid development of FRET-based sensors for bacterial detection, most of them are not appropriate for point-of-care detection. Considering the practical implications for FRET-based bacterial detection, research efforts aimed at point-of-care testing are warranted. For example, the smartphone system is combined with the detection platform to achieve point-of-care testing, which can intuitively show the results and greatly save manpower and material resources. This requires the detection platform to have high sensitivity, anti-interference ability, signal output stability, and other characteristics.

Numerous efforts in this field have led us to believe that FRET-based detection sensors hold great promise. Near-infrared fluorescent materials are currently the focus of research because of their advantages of relatively deep tissue penetration and the ability to shield biological tissue from autofluorescence interference. Simultaneous detection of multiple analytes is also one of the development directions, which requires distinct output signals. Therefore, fluorescent probes with narrow spectra or in combination with logic gates and other methods have been developed to achieve simultaneous detection. These challenges and issues also reveal great promise for the development of the FRET field. We believe that as more people gain insight into the great advantages of FRET tools, these problems will also be solved and FRET will be further developed.

Wanqing Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Weiqiang Li: Writing – review & editing. Yang Song: Writing – review & editing. Qian Xu: Writing – review & editing. Hengyi Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
